content:
The Evolution Most Senior Devs Quietly Make

10 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I still remember the exact moment the concentric circles lost their magic.
It was a routine pull request. A small change to a reporting feature. Nothing exotic. Yet the diff touched five projects: Domain, Application, Infrastructure, the feature’s own contracts project, and the API layer. The actual business logic was maybe twelve lines. Everything else was ceremony — interfaces that existed only to satisfy the dependency rule, mapping classes that existed only to keep the core “pure,” and a unit test that mocked three abstractions just to assert that a number was returned correctly.
I approved the PR. Then I sat there for a minute, staring at the solution explorer, and realized I no longer believed the structure was helping.
That quiet discomfort is more common than most of us admit. We start with Hexagonal, Onion, or Clean Architecture because they feel clarifying. They give us language, diagrams, and a sense of professional rigor. Years later, many of the same developers who once defended the pure form begin organizing code by feature instead of by layer, occasionally letting infrastructure concerns leak into places the original diagrams forbade, and feeling slightly guilty about it.
